blogs.title: The NetMirror Deception
blogs.content:
My Deep Dive into NetMirror

I saw some of my friends using this app called NetMirror. If you don’t know it, it’s an APK for watching pirated movies. The icon was clean, the UI is smooth, and the movies actually played without any lag. now who would not use this app .

But something felt really off to me.

Think about it—a free streaming app that isn't on the Play Store, no official website, always changing its domains . Just a random link that is shared with by my brother to watch loki .

I have tust issues btw, so instead of installing it on my actual phone, I decided to take it apart. I threw it into my Kali Linux environment to see what is happening inside the code.

What I found after a few hours of digging was not just some ads. It was a very smart, carefully made spyware. i truly admire the creater of this app. This app was literally designed to trick the security tools .

In this post, I will show you the "red flags" I found in the code, and why you should tell delete it rightt now

Researched by: addssh

Tools used: Gemini, Perplexity, Claude, Stack Overflow

How I Did It: My Lab Setup

Before jumping into the crazy things I found, I should explain my process. I didn't want this thing anywhere near my personal data, so I used Static Analysis. This means I just looked at the without installing it in my mobile phone. I kept everything locked inside an isolated Kali Linux virtual machine just to be safe.

Everything I used is free and comes standard with Kali. If you're curious, here is the toolkit I used to break it down:

  • apktool: I used this to decompile the APK and dig into the raw folders.
  • jadx: This is used for converting java code into human readable format
  • grep: This is how I hunted for specific red flags like 'password' or 'SMS' across thousands of files.
  • Base64 decoder: To reveal the secret web addresses that were scrambled to hide from scanners.
  • VirusTotal / URLScan: My final check to see what the rest of the security world thinks of the domains I found.

honestly its easy too what you can find with just some basic tools and a bit of curiosity. You don't need a degree to see that

Step 1: The Manifest — Hunting for Red Flags

Whenever I start looking at a suspicious app, the first place I go is the AndroidManifest.xml file. This is like apps passport its shows us what are the permissions that are given to the apk file. For a movie app, it should be simple, Just internet and maybe storage.

But when I ran a grep command on NetMirror's manifest, I saw things that definitely didn't belong in a streaming service:

Let me break down why these permissions made me worried:

  • WRITE_SETTINGS: This is a big deal. It gives the app power to change your actual system settings. While a normal movie app might change brightness while you watch a film, it does that inside the app. It doesn't need system-level access. Malware loves this permission because it can keep your phone awake in the background to do 'dirty work' like clicking ads or syncing data while you aren't even using it.
  • ACCESS_WIFI_STATE: This lets the app see your network name and connection info. When hackers combine this with your device ID, they can build a 'fingerprint' to track exactly who and where you are across different networks.
  • BIND_GET_INSTALL_REFERRER_SERVICE: This is a sneaky one. It tracks which link or Telegram group you used to download the APK. It’s basically a tracking tool so the developers can see which of their tricks are working best to get people to install their spyware.
  • usesCleartextTraffic="true": I found this buried in the code too. It basically tells the phone, 'Hey, it's okay to send data without encryption.' This is huge for a privacy-minded person because it means anything the app sends can be read by anyone else on the same Wi-Fi.the attack i m talking about is called MITM

But as I go deeper, I found out it was actually much smarter at hiding than I thought.

Step 2: The Sneaky Architecture

This is where I started to realize how clever the developers actually was. Usually, when you scan an app for viruses, the tools look at the Java code. That is where most Android apps keep their secrets. But when I looked at the Java side of NetMirror, everything seemed... normal.

It turns out NetMirror is built using React Native. In these kinds of apps, the Java code is just an empty shell. The real 'brain'—all the actual logic and the malicious stuff—is hidden in a single file called assets/index.android.bundle. Even crazier? It’s compiled into something called Hermes bytecode.

hermes-dec is a reverse-engineering tool which can be used for disassembling and decompiling React Native files compiled into the Hermes VM bytecode (HBC) format. It's a massive blind spot that the hackers exploited on purpose.before i run an automated scan to see if i can find any malicious code but as expected it failed, that’s why hackers love manual work

To see what was actually going on, I couldn't just read the code like a book. I asked gemini for help than it told me to use the strings command to pull out readable text from that binary file and manually hunt through the garbage. That’s when the 'interesting' stuff started showing up in red.

Step 3: Cracking the Secret Servers

After digging into that JavaScript bundle, I hit the jackpot. I found a bunch of weird-looking strings that didn’t look like normal code. They were Base64 encoded—which is basically a way to encrypted text so it’s not immediately obvious to a human or a basic scanner.

When I put these strings into a decoder, I found the true heart of the spyware. It wasn't talking to movie servers; it was talking to these:

I found five different domains, all using the name 'MobiDetect'. Here is the list I uncovered:

  • https://mobidetects.live
  • https://mobidetects.store
  • https://mobidetects.pro
  • https://mobidetect.app
  • https://mobidetect.art

I did a little more digging and found that all of these were registered on the exact same day. Even weirder, they were all using 'domain parking' pages to look like they were empty or for sale. This is a classic move to fool web security scanners.

Think about it: why would a 'free movie app' need to hide its web addresses in a secret code? There is no technical reason for a legitimate app to do this. The only reason is to hide from security tools. This isn't just a mistake; it is all planned.

Step 5: The Permission trap

I was just on call with my friend he told me that he never allowed some the permissions it means he is on the safe side , right? So I went back to the JavaScript bundle and ran another grep search, and what I found honestly shocked me. I discovered some of the most dangerous permissions possible sitting right there in the code—permissions that have absolutely no reason to be in a movie app.

The scary part? These were not in the AndroidManifest.xml file I checked earlier. If they were, nobody would ever install the app, and the Play Store would flag it instantly. Instead, they are hidden deep in the JavaScript, waiting to be triggered later.

This is what researchers call a 'staged attack.' Here is how it works:

  • Phase 1: You install the app. It looks normal and only asks for basic things. It spends a few days 'fingerprinting' your phone and sending your data to the MobiDetect servers.
  • Phase 2: If the hackers decide your phone is a good target, the app suddenly wakes up. You might see a random popup asking for SMS access, maybe lying to you saying it’s for 'account verification' or a 'new update.'
  • Phase 3: The moment you click 'Allow,' it’s game over. In the terms of hacking we call it social engineering- The process where you play with psychology of your victim. Now,They can read your private texts, see who you call, and even intercept two-factor authentication (2FA) codes from your bank.

By the time you see that request, the app has already been on your device long enough to know everything about you.

Step 7: Putting It All Together

After looking at the code over and over again. Everything—the domains, the code strings, and the libraries—points to one name: 'MobiDetect.' Whether this is a known tracking tool or something custom-made, one thing is clear: it is built for total surveillance.

Here is exactly what this app is doing behind your back while you watch a movie:

  • Permanent Identity Theft (Fingerprinting): The app uses a library called RNDeviceInfo to steal your IMEI, SIM serial number, and Android ID. It even checks your battery level and if your headphones are plugged in! This creates a 'shadow profile' of your phone. Even if you uninstall the app, they will recognize your device the next time you install any of their other malicious apps.
  • Stealing Your Passwords (Credential Scraping): This is the scariest part. The app can inject secret JavaScript into its built-in browser. If you type a password or select a private message, it uses window.getSelection() to grab that text and send it to their servers. I even found code in the native C++ library specifically designed to steal usernames and passwords from login pages.
  • Ad Fraud (Invisible Clicks): I found code like dispatchTouchEvent which lets the app 'touch' things on the screen without you seeing it. It uses this to click on invisible ads in the background. This makes money for the hackers while draining your battery and using up your mobile data.

When you look at these things one by one, you might think it's just 'aggressive tracking.' But when you put it all together—the sandbox evasion, the hidden servers, and the password theft—there is no other word for it. This is professional spyware. NetMirror isn't a free service; it’s a trap.
